title: SQL Introduction
description: Learn the basics of SQL — the language for managing and querying relational databases.

sections.title: What is SQL?
sections.lesson:
SQL (Structured Query Language) is the standard language for communicating with relational database management systems (RDBMS). It lets you create, read, update, and delete data in structured tables.

SQL is used by nearly every application that needs persistent data storage — from simple web apps to enterprise systems.

Popular databases that use SQL:

  • MySQL / MariaDB
  • PostgreSQL
  • SQLite
  • Microsoft SQL Server
  • Oracle Database

SQL operations fall into categories:

CategoryCommandsPurpose
DQLSELECTQuery/read data
DMLINSERT, UPDATE, DELETEModify data
DDLCREATE, ALTER, DROPDefine structure
DCLGRANT, REVOKEControl access

questions.question: What does SQL stand for?
questions.explanation: SQL stands for Structured Query Language. It is the standard language for relational database management systems.

questions.question: Which SQL command is used to READ/retrieve data from a table?
questions.explanation: SELECT is the SQL command for querying and retrieving data from one or more tables. It belongs to DQL (Data Query Language).

sections.title: The SELECT Statement
sections.lesson:
The SELECT statement retrieves data from one or more tables. It is the most frequently used SQL command.

-- Select all columns from a table
SELECT * FROM students;

-- Select specific columns
SELECT name, grade FROM students;

-- Select with a column alias
SELECT name AS student_name, grade AS score FROM students;

-- Select with ORDER BY (sort results)
SELECT name, grade FROM students ORDER BY grade DESC;

-- Select with LIMIT (cap results)
SELECT name FROM students ORDER BY grade DESC LIMIT 5;

Useful SELECT clauses:

ClausePurposeExample
ORDER BYSort resultsORDER BY name ASC
LIMITCap the number of rowsLIMIT 10
DISTINCTRemove duplicate rowsSELECT DISTINCT city
ASAlias a column namegrade AS score

questions.question: What does SELECT * FROM students return?
choices: All columns and all rows from the students table
questions.answer: All columns and all rows from the students table
questions.explanation: The * wildcard means 'all columns'. Without a WHERE clause, all rows are returned. So SELECT * FROM students returns every column and row.

questions.question: Which clause sorts the result set in SQL?
questions.explanation: ORDER BY sorts the result set. Use ASC for ascending (default) or DESC for descending order.

questions.question: What does SELECT DISTINCT city FROM offices return?
questions.explanation: DISTINCT eliminates duplicate values in the result. If five offices are in 'Manila', only one 'Manila' appears in the output.

sections.title: The WHERE Clause
sections.lesson:
The WHERE clause filters rows — only rows matching the condition are returned.

-- Exact match
SELECT * FROM students WHERE grade = 90;

-- Comparison
SELECT name FROM students WHERE grade >= 85;

-- String match
SELECT * FROM students WHERE section = 'BSCS-3A';

-- LIKE pattern matching
SELECT * FROM students WHERE name LIKE 'A%';   -- starts with A
SELECT * FROM students WHERE name LIKE '%son'; -- ends with 'son'

-- IN operator (multiple values)
SELECT * FROM students WHERE section IN ('BSCS-3A', 'BSCS-3B');

-- BETWEEN (range)
SELECT * FROM students WHERE grade BETWEEN 80 AND 90;

-- Combining conditions
SELECT * FROM students WHERE grade >= 85 AND section = 'BSCS-3A';
SELECT * FROM students WHERE grade < 75 OR absent > 3;

LIKE wildcards:

  • % — matches any sequence of characters
  • _ — matches exactly one character

questions.question: Which SQL clause is used to filter rows based on a condition?
questions.explanation: WHERE filters rows in a query. Only rows where the condition is true are included in the result.

questions.question: What does WHERE name LIKE 'A%' match?
questions.explanation: In SQL LIKE patterns, % matches any sequence of characters. 'A%' means 'starts with A followed by anything'.

questions.question: Which operator checks if a value falls within a range (inclusive)?
questions.explanation: BETWEEN low AND high returns rows where the value is >= low AND <= high. Both endpoints are inclusive.

sections.title: INSERT, UPDATE, DELETE
sections.lesson:
These DML commands modify the data in your tables.

INSERT — Add new rows:

-- Insert one row
INSERT INTO students (student_id, name, grade, section)
VALUES (1001, 'Alice Cruz', 92, 'BSCS-3A');

-- Insert multiple rows
INSERT INTO students (student_id, name, grade)
VALUES (1002, 'Bob Santos', 88),
       (1003, 'Carol Reyes', 95);

UPDATE — Modify existing rows:

-- Update a specific row
UPDATE students SET grade = 95 WHERE student_id = 1001;

-- Update multiple columns
UPDATE students
SET grade = 90, section = 'BSCS-3B'
WHERE name = 'Bob Santos';

DELETE — Remove rows:

-- Delete a specific row
DELETE FROM students WHERE student_id = 1003;

-- Delete all rows (keep table structure)
DELETE FROM students;

Warning: Always use WHERE with UPDATE and DELETE — without it, all rows are affected!

questions.question: Which SQL command adds new rows to a table?
questions.explanation: INSERT INTO table (...) VALUES (...) adds new rows to a table. You specify the columns and the corresponding values.

questions.question: What happens if you run UPDATE students SET grade = 0 without a WHERE clause?
choices: Only the first student is updated
questions.explanation: Without a WHERE clause, UPDATE affects every row in the table. This is a common mistake that can cause data loss.

questions.question: Which command removes rows from a table but keeps the table structure intact?
questions.explanation: DELETE removes rows from a table but keeps the table itself. DROP TABLE removes the entire table. TRUNCATE also removes all rows but is non-transactional.

sections.title: JOINs — Combining Tables
sections.lesson:
A JOIN combines rows from two or more tables based on a related column (usually a foreign key).

Given two tables:

students
student_idname
1001Alice
1002Bob
grades
grade_idstudent_idscore
1100192
2100288

INNER JOIN — Returns only matching rows from both tables:

SELECT s.name, g.score
FROM students s
INNER JOIN grades g ON s.student_id = g.student_id;

LEFT JOIN — Returns all rows from the left table, plus matching rows from the right (NULL if no match):

SELECT s.name, g.score
FROM students s
LEFT JOIN grades g ON s.student_id = g.student_id;
-- Includes students with no grade (score will be NULL)

When to use which:

  • Use INNER JOIN when you only want rows that have matches in both tables
  • Use LEFT JOIN when you want all records from the left table, regardless of matches

questions.question: Which JOIN returns only rows that have matching values in both tables?
questions.explanation: INNER JOIN returns only the rows where the join condition is satisfied in both tables. Rows without a match are excluded.

questions.question: What does LEFT JOIN return that INNER JOIN does not?
choices: All rows from the left table, even without matches
All rows from the right table
questions.answer: All rows from the left table, even without matches
questions.explanation: LEFT JOIN returns all rows from the left table. For rows with no match in the right table, the right-side columns are filled with NULL.

questions.question: In a JOIN, what does the ON clause specify?
choices: The condition linking the two tables
questions.answer: The condition linking the two tables
questions.explanation: The ON clause defines how the two tables are related — typically matching a foreign key in one table to the primary key in the other.
